claims.claim: The optimum operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries is from 20°C to 40°C, and the temperature difference should be kept within 5°C.
claims.quote: The optimum operating temperature range of LIBs is from 20℃ to 40℃ [3–5], and the temperature difference should be kept within 5 ℃ [6].

claims.claim: Too low temperature increases the internal resistance and polarization internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries, increases power and energy loss, and reduces discharge capacity; too high temperature increases the degradation rate, reducing performance and cycle life.
claims.quote: Too low temperature will increase the internal resistance and polarization internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries, increase power and energy loss, and reduce the discharge capacity of the battery; too high temperature will increase the degradation rate of the battery, thereby reducing the performance and cycle life [2].

claims.claim: Metal foam can extend the total convection heat transfer area by nearly 10-fold, thereby significantly reducing the maximum temperature of batteries.
claims.quote: metal foam can extend the total convection heat transfer area by nearly 10-fold, thereby significantly reducing the maximum temperature of batteries.

claims.claim: When cooling air flows through metal foam, flow resistance increases significantly at low mass flow rate, causing relatively poor temperature difference of batteries.
claims.quote: it is inevitable that the flow resistance across the metal foam will increase significantly, which will cause relatively poor temperature difference of batteries at a low mass flow rate

claims.claim: Along the cooling air flow direction, the downstream temperature of the battery module is significantly higher than its upstream temperature, because cooling air absorbs heat continuously and its cooling capacity declines.
claims.quote: Along the cooling air flow direction, the downstream temperature of the battery module is significantly higher than its upstream temperature. This is because the cooling air will continue to absorb heat in the process of heat dissipation, resulting in its own cooling capacity decline, which makes the temperature of the battery show a trend of increasing along the direction of cooling air flow.

claims.claim: When insulation layer thickness is increased to 0.7 mm, TRP is ceased in both in-line and brick module configurations.
claims.quote: When the insulting layer thickness was further increased to 0.7 mm, the TRP was ceased for in both types of modules.

claims.claim: At insulation thickness of 0.6 mm, TRP is inhibited in the brick module but still occurs in the in-line module with a TRP time interval of approximately 1096 s.
claims.quote: It could be observed that the chain propagation of TR has been effectively inhibited in the brick module while it still occurred in the in-line module, whereas accompanied by a longer TRP time interval of roughly 1096 s.

claims.claim: When insulation layer thickness exceeds 2 mm, TRP can be completely ceased even when local failure of insulation occurs.
claims.quote: As the thickness increased to 2 mm, the number of TR cells was found to not improve and propagation has been prevented.

claims.claim: Local failure of thermal insulation initially leads to small-scale TR within the local scope, which contributes to larger heat accumulation and raises the parameter threshold needed to prevent TRP.
claims.quote: The local failure of inhibition strategy will initially lead to the small-scale battery TR within local scope, contributing a larger heat accumulation and potentiality of TRP.

claims.claim: When air velocity increases from 1 m/s to 4 m/s with 0.5 mm insulation, chain propagation of TR is effectively inhibited in both in-line and brick battery pack configurations.
claims.quote: As the air velocity was further increased to 4 m⋅s−1, the chain propagation of TR has been effectively inhibited in both types of battery packs.

claims.claim: TR propagation to Bat 2 is prevented when the TR triggering temperature TTR,ARC is raised above 469 °C, which is the maximum temperature that the front edges of cells inside Bat 2 can reach.
claims.quote: suppose that TTR,ARC is higher than 469 °C, TR propagation will be prevented because T}2_f(t) and T}2_b(t) will never reach TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: Delaying TR propagation from Bat 1 to Bat 2 leads to a higher temperature in Bat 2, which shortens the TR propagation time from Bat 2 to Bat 3 (D2→3).
claims.quote: a delay in the TR propagation from Bat 1 to Bat 2 may lead to a higher temperature in Bat 2, which leads to a much faster TR propagation from Bat 2 to Bat 3.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when the heat dissipation coefficient hdis exceeds 70 W m⁻² K⁻¹, because max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} only reaches 249 °C, which is below TTR,ARC.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when hdis is larger than 70 W m−2 K−1, because the max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t) can only reach 249 °C < TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when a 1 mm thermal resistant layer with thermal conductivity less than 0.2 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹ is inserted between adjacent batteries, limiting max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} to 226 °C, which is below TTR,ARC.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when kD equals or less than 0.2 W m−2 K−1, because the max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)} can only reach 226 °C < TTR,ARC.

claims.claim: TR propagation is prevented when DHe is reduced to 75% or less of its original value, as shown by the condition on max{T}2_f(t), T}2_b(t)}.
claims.quote: TR propagation will be prevented when DHe equals 75% or less of its original value.
